sentences.text: My name is Lillian Eckstein.
Maiden name, Mendelson.
I was born in Czechoslovakia, Nizni Verecky, 1926.
Well, we had lived in a small town, and we had around 80 people in our family.
Parents, aunts, uncles, sisters, and brothers.
And we-- the people in those days had large families.
And we were a very happy family.
We were not rich, but we were quite comfortable.
We had a feeling of being loved.
We felt that we belonged.
And it-- we grew up feeling with security and with love.
Yes.
My parents had eight children, and-- ranging from when the war broke out, from age eight to 29.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My older sister was married.
The second to the next was married, had children.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And--
Yes.
The oldest was Margaret.
Her name was [?
Mantzi. ?] She was married to a medic, and she had three children.
She lived in Czechoslovakia, in [INAUDIBLE].
The next one was Ibi.
She was married.
Her husband was brutally murdered during the war.
She had two children.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [INAUDIBLE].
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In 1938, the Hungarians came into Czechoslovakia.
Well, then I had a sister, Ibi, and then I had a sister, Ella.
And then I had a-- and then I came.
Then my brother came before me.
Then I had two other brothers who came after me, and a sister.
Their names were Jacob, Menache, Gabriel, Ibi, [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE], Ella, and Lilly.
No, it wasn't a big place.
It was a large town, and they had small towns around it.
And the people, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all kinds of nationalities, they lived happy, peaceful together until the disaster came.
Yes.
Well, the town has changed three times names.
The last one was the Soviet Union came in.
We were not there at that time.
And the name was changed to Nyzhni Vorota.
I don't know exact spelling for that.
OK.
The people lived in peace and harmony together--- Gentiles, Jews.
My best friends were Gentiles who lived next door.
We were school friends.
We celebrated holidays together.
We didn't know what it meant to-- to be isolated as Jews.
Only when the Hungarians and the Germans came in, that's when the people in town were afraid of us.
That we were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] outcasts, misfits, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] troublemakers, swindlers.
They classified us as the scum of the earth.
Yes.
My grandfather [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] was a great rabbi.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Excuse me.
[PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] Those days, they did not have the courts, so when people had disputes, he was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] working as a judge.
When they had marital problems, other problems.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he had a brutal, brutal end.
They caught him.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They tore his beard out.
I saw it.
They kicked him.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Excuse me.
[CRYING] And he died.
The German gestapo.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He was a pious, a religious man.
He never hurt anybody.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] OK.
Yeah.
My grandfather was respected by all of us.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- we grew up respecting our parents, our oldest.
And we obeyed.
And perhaps this led me to believe that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not everybody is bad.
And I managed to keep this with me for the rest of my life, that there are good and bad people in this world.
And I want to believe that people are not bad after all what happened to me.
Yes.
They all lived in the same block, three generations.
Well, they celebrated together.
And in Christmastime, I celebrated my friends' holidays, and they celebrated our holidays.
Our families were together.
And as mentioned before, we just had a feeling of belonging, of being loved.
And that is more important than anything in the world, knowing that you belong.
Yes, we did.
Well, we had Pesach.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a Jewish holiday.
We had Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur.
We had all other holidays.
Shabbat every Friday.
We celebrated Shabbat every week.
It was a very joyous time to relax, two families together.
They-- they were reading poems, stories.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We had no television, so therefore, we were spending a lot of time together with the families.
And it was a very healthy thing to do.
Yes.
It was-- it started Friday night, and it ended Saturday when the sun went down, when the stars came out.
That's when it ended.
It was a very joyous time.
We all looked forward to the Shabbat [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because we spent together time with the family and with our friends.
Well, Friday night we had our meal, and then we sang songs around the table.
Well, we had fish, soup-- chicken soup-- and chicken, and-- and dessert.
And after the meal, we would sing songs, tell stories, listen-- the parents would listen to the children, what they have to say.
And everybody had a chance to-- to talk.
And so we went to sleep with a-- with a great deal of love and, as I said, a feeling of belonging, of being wanted.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that carried us through until this day.
My father was a lumber dealer.
That part of Czechoslovakia, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a lot of wood was growing to build houses, to make furniture.
And he was a dealer.
He would buy the merchandise, sell it in Prague.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] OK?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we managed to-- managed to live quite comfortable.
We were not rich.
As a matter of fact, I had to wear my sister's shoes, because we-- there was, apparently, not enough money to buy new clothes.
And we didn't mind.
In the house, we spoke Yiddish.
In the school, we spoke Czechoslovakia.
And the-- some of the neighbors were Hungarians.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they were not Russians, but they were Carpathians.
So we grew up with four languages.
And-- and then, in 1938, when the Hungarians came in, we were forced to learn Hungarian fluently in school.
And so we had to pick up another language, which was five.
It was Yiddish, Czech, Carpathian, Russ-- which is part of Russia-- and Hungarian.
They were-- some of them were Czechoslovakians.
Some of them were Russian descent from the First World War.
Some of them were Hungarians.
And-- and the Jews.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They all lived in harmony.
There was never any problem there, until they started instigating and manipulating the people against the Jews.

Towards-- towards the end, when the children came in and they were trying to isolate themselves from us because they were told that we are dangerous people, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and that we-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we use blood in Pesach for certain ceremonies.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In Pesach, they use certain wines to pray and all that.
So we-- I didn't understand what was going on, and I couldn't figure out-- and my friends couldn't explain to me.
They just told-- they were told to stay away from us.
And it was very painful to me because I did not get an explanation why.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
I-- I guess that they had to obey their parents or the authorities who gave them all the orders to do so.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [SIGHS] Excuse me.
When things started changing, I was around 10, 11 when the Hungarians came.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we-- it started slowly.
And then towards the end, we lost most of our friends, not because they wanted to, but they were told to do so.
As a matter of fact, some of them were trying to hide us in their homes.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But we wanted to be together, so we all went to the ghetto together.
Well, I didn't know-- didn't know the consequences.
All I saw is that the police-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the police looked different.
The management in town looked different.
And the-- every once in awhile, they used to pick up a Jew and beat him up for no good reason, just to instigate the problems.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's how it started.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
And also, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] as time went on, the Gentile friends were afraid to socialize with us, and so they stayed away.
Bay.
They stepped aside, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not because they wanted to.
They were afraid of their own shadow, what's going to happen to them?
So that's how we lost them.
What happened in school?
We were [? pecked. ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were thrown-- they were throwing stones at us, calling us dirty Jew.
Get out of town, and all that stuff.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We used to come home-- I came home one day.
A stone hit me, and the blood was just dripping down.
And my mother grabbed me, and told me to keep quiet and just washed off my blood and told us to stay indoors.
And so these little minor things came about.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And nobody could say anything.
We didn't know that we can-- we should fight back, or we should do something.
But we were led like little animals.
We-- we just followed what-- we were-- we did what we were told to do.
But today, we know better.
My father was taken to Munkatabor.
It's a work-- forced labor camp, OK?
And they took away-- away the breadwinner from the house.
And so we had no way of living.
We were very, very bad off.
We were starved-- starving, practically.
Because those days, nobody had a bank book or nobody knew what it means to have [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] set aside for a rainy day, as they would say in this country.
So we-- we just had no-- we had practically no food in the house for eight children.
And it was a pretty bad time.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so when the Germans came--
Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were some Gentile neighbors.
They realized what was going on, and they were sneaking in bread, potatoes, or milk.
They had their own cows.
And we were lucky if we had one meal a day.
And so we survived.
I don't know their names.
As a matter of fact, I went back this summer to Czechoslovakia.
And I wanted to look up these people, because I wanted to help them in any way I could.
But I-- I was told not to go into town because of the political reasons, what went on there this summer, the separation from Slovak-- Slovensko from Czechoslovakia.
And I didn't want to take a chance to go.
They were very dear.
These Gentile friends, they were like my sisters and brothers.
I did not know the difference between them.
And it was not their fault that they turned against us, because as I've said before, they were afraid of their own life.
And of course, there were some people who deliberately waited to chase us out from our homes so they can take over.
And they so-- they-- they succeeded.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because when we left, we left with what we had on our backs, the clothes on our backs.
Everything was left behind.
The Gendarme-- this police-- came and they took him.
They took him into Poland, and it was a forced labor camp.
They took him to work.
Not only him, but the rest of the people.
Not only from that town, but from all-- all over there.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when he came back, he was unrecognizable from there.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] He finally wound up in Auschwitz, in the gas chamber.
As I said, they were sneaking in food.
Whatever they-- they were able to exchange for food, they-- people would come in and take whatever was any-- of any value, and they brought food for that.
Yes.
Well, the Germans came in.
1943 they came in.
And they gave us 24 hours to clear out from there.
So they all con-- they all congregated in Mukacevo, in a ghetto.
All the people from around-- Mukacevo was a big city, and all the people from around the area was congregated.
It was a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was a-- outside the city, it was a lumber yard, and they made-- the military used to be there.
They cleared out, and they put all these people in.
Whole families, everybody was there.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] little by little, they started emptying them out and put them in the cattle trains and take them to Auschwitz.
The warning signals were, the-- the people were bewildered.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They didn't know what was happening because something like this-- nobody anticipated that something like this can happen.
There were rumors going around that they were taking people into Germany to work-- to work.
But nobody ever knew [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the consequences.
Nobody anticipated that there are crama-- crematoriums and concentration camps.
The rumors went around that we are going to go to work, to Germany.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the rumors were spread by the German Gestapo so the people wouldn't try to fight back.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so we went along.
Whatever we were told to do, we did it.
The worst part-- we were in that ghetto and the food was scarce.
We were with buses, with-- with-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not donkeys.
What do you call these wagons where they have-- every possible way.
By buses, by train, whichever way they-- they transported us.
On trucks.
The worst part of it, what I will never forget as long-- as long as I live, my youngest brother [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] was in ghetto.
And-- and he was just starving, like all of us.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Apparently, he couldn't take it.
And so he went to my mother and-- and grabbed her by the foot and looked at her and he said, please, give me something to eat because I can't take it anymore.
So she turned to him and she said, I have no-- nothing to give you.
And he said, please.
I want to die.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SOBBING] And I can never forget the look on my mother's face.
[SOBBING] And she looked at him [SOBBING] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] it was-- it was horrible.
Excuse me, please.
Excuse me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And somebody kicked him from the back.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he said, please let me die.
[SOBBING] Give me some bread.
[CRYING] Excuse me.
Excuse me, please.
